folio.label: 176r
folio.l:
There is an olde ¸uäbe quod she , seitú ; æat the goodnesse æ
æè maist do this day , do it

and a bide nat , ne delay it nat
til to morwe ,

And æäfore I cou~seille , æ ©e sende ©oure messa-
geres , swicú as ben discrete & wyse ,

vn to ©oure aduäsaries ,
tellynge he~ on ©oure bihalue ,

æ if they woln trete of pees &
of a cord ,

æ they shape he~ w outen delay or tariynge , to
come vn to vs ,
whicú thyngö ¹fourmed was in dede ,,

And
whan theise trespassours & repentynge folk of here folies , æ
is to seyn ; the aduäsaries of Melibe ,

hadden herd what theise
Messageres seiden vn to he~ ,

they weren rigút glad & ioyeful ,
And answerden ful mekely & benygnely ,

©eldynge graces &
thankynges to here lord Melibe , & to al his compaignye

&
shopen he~ w outen delay , to go witú the Messagers & obeye ,
to the comau~dement of here lord Melibe ,

And rigút a noon æei
token here weye to the court of Melibe ,

& token witú he~
so~me of here trewe frendes , to make feitú for he~ , and for
to ben here borwes

¢ And whan they were come to the pä-
sence of Melibe , he seide he~ theise wordes

¢ It stant thus
quod Melibe , & sootú it is , that ©e

causeles & w outen skyle
& resou~ ,

han doon grete Iniuries & wronges to me and to
my wyf , Pèdence , and to my dougúter also ,

for ©e han entred
in to myn hous by violence ,

and haue don swicú outrage
æ alle men knowen wel , that ©e han deserued the detú ,

&
æäfore wol I knowe & wite of ©ow ,

wheither ©e wol putte æe
punysshynge & chastisynge , & the vengeau~ce of this outge
in the wil of me & of my wyf , or ©e wol nat

¢ Thanne
the wysest of hem thre , answered for he~ alle , & seide , ,,

¢ Sire quod he , we knowen wel , æ we ben vnworthy , to co-
me to the court of so gret a lord , & so worthy as ©e ben ,

For
we han so gretly mystaken vs , & han offendid & a gilt in
swicú a wyse a geyn ©oure heye lordship~ ,

æ trewely we han
deserued the deetú ,

But ©et for the grete goodnesse & debo-
nairetee , æ al the world wytnessetú of ©oure ¹sone ,

we sb~-
mitten vs to the excellence & benygnytee of ©oure gcious
lordshipe ,

& ben redy to obeye , to alle ©oure comaundeme~-
tes ,

bysekynge ©ow , æ of ©oure mäciable pitee , ©e wol consi-
dere oure grete repentaunce & lowe submissiou~ ,

& gunte
vs for©euenesse of oure outgeous trespas & offence ,

For wel
we knowen , æ ©oure liberal gce & mäcy , strecchen he~ foræerö
in to goodnesse , than doon oure outgeous giltes & trespas
in to wykkednesse ,

al be it that cursedly & dampnablely ,
we han a gilt a geyn ©oure heye lordshipe

¢ Thanne
Melibe tok he~ vp fro the g¼und , ful benygnely ,
& receyued
